title: Tracheostomy Tube Obstruction
chief_complaint: Nursing home patient with chronic tracheostomy. Nurse calls: 'His trach tube came out and he's struggling to breathe. He's turning blue.'

physical_exam.Airway: Tracheostomy tube dislodged, lying on patient's chest. Stoma visible but narrowing. Patient using accessory muscles, severe respiratory distress. Cyanotic lips. No air movement through mouth or nose (laryngectomy patient - ONLY breathes through stoma!).

hidden_data.surgical_history: Total laryngectomy 2 years ago for laryngeal cancer. Permanent tracheostomy. No upper airway connection (cannot intubate or ventilate from above).
hidden_data.cxr: No pneumothorax. ET tube not present.

correct_diagnosis: Tracheostomy Tube Dislodgement in Laryngectomy Patient - ACUTE AIRWAY EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Laryngectomy patients = NO connection between upper airway and lungs. CANNOT be intubated or ventilated through mouth/nose. ONLY breathe through stoma. This is CRITICAL knowledge - attempting oral/nasal intubation will fail and delay life-saving intervention

Tracheostomy emergency algorithm: 1) Check trach tube patency (suction, deflate cuff, try to pass suction catheter). 2) If obstructed: Remove inner cannula (if dual-cannula). 3) If still obstructed: Deflate cuff and remove entire tube. 4) If tube out and stoma patent: Insert new tracheostomy tube (same size or smaller). 5) If cannot insert: Insert smaller tube (ET tube can be used as temporizing measure through stoma). 6) If stoma closing/narrowed: Insert bougie or suction catheter as guide, railroad new tube over it. 7) If cannot secure stoma: Oral intubation (ONLY if NOT laryngectomy - check for air movement from mouth)

For laryngectomy patients (NO upper airway): If stoma closes and cannot cannulate → EMERGENT revision of stoma (scalpel incision through stoma into trachea - can be life-saving). Bag-mask ventilation over stoma (pediatric mask fits better). Oxygen via stoma

Prevention: Trach ties secure (1 finger snug). Humidification (prevents mucous plugging). Regular suctioning. Spare trach tube at bedside. Emergency equipment at bedside: Spare trach tube (same size + one smaller), suction, bag-mask, oxygen. All staff caring for trach patients must be trained in emergency management
